items.title: Profile at realistic array sizes
items.avoid: Micro-benchmarking on 10-element toy arrays and concluding loops are fine for production pipelines.
items.prefer: Time operations on batch sizes you actually train with (e.g. 50k–1M rows) using `timeit` or notebook `%timeit`.
items.why: Vectorization overhead only pays off past a scale threshold; ML prep almost always hits that threshold.
items.example: Compare `for i in range(n): ...` vs vectorized code with `n = X.shape[0]` from your real CSV, not `n = 8`.

items.title: Make broadcasting explicit when semantics matter
items.avoid: Relying on implicit trailing-dimension alignment when subtracting per-row vs per-column statistics.
items.prefer: Use `[:, np.newaxis]` or `reshape(-1, 1)` to document intent; assert shapes with `assert arr.shape == expected`.
items.why: Explicit axes prevent silent feature corruption — one of the costliest bugs in data prep.
items.example: Per-row demeaning: `X - X.mean(axis=1, keepdims=True)`, not `X - X.mean(axis=0)` unless you intend column means.

items.title: Keep arrays contiguous and typed once
items.avoid: Repeated `astype`, `tolist`, and list-of-lists conversions between pipeline stages.
items.prefer: Convert DataFrame numeric blocks with `.to_numpy(dtype=np.float64)` once; chain vectorized ops on that buffer.
items.why: Contiguous float buffers maximize ufunc and BLAS throughput into downstream ML libraries.
items.example: `X = df.select_dtypes('number').to_numpy(dtype=np.float64)` then all transforms in-place or on views of `X`.

items.title: Use masks for conditional updates
items.avoid: Branching with `if` inside Python loops over array indices.
items.prefer: Build boolean masks with comparisons (`arr > 0`) and assign with `arr[mask] = value` or `np.where`.
items.why: Masks keep conditional logic in compiled paths and read like the math (`indicator = y == 1`).
items.example: Zero negative predictions: `preds = np.where(preds < 0, 0.0, preds)` across the whole batch.
